title: Common Sense (Paine)
description: Common Sense (Paine) — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "常识" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Overview of Thomas Paine''s Common Sense (1776), the revolutionary pamphlet that made the case for American independence.'

memes.label: 'Absolute Government'
memes.description: 'Paine''s critique of governments in which power is concentrated without legal restraint — the King is law rather than the reverse'

memes.label: Africa
memes.description: 'The continent invoked by Paine as a cautionary example of European colonialism and barbarism'

memes.label: America
memes.description: 'The central political cause of Common Sense; Paine treats America not merely as a geographic territory but as a moral and political project whose independence from Britain is both a natural right and a historical necessity'

memes.label: American Revolution
memes.description: 'The political and military struggle through which the American colonies achieved independence from Britain; the central event of Common Sense'

memes.label: "Appendix"
memes.description: "Appendix to Common Sense, with an address to the people called Quakers"

memes.label: Asia
memes.description: 'The continent referenced by Paine alongside Africa as proof of Britain''s global reach and declining influence'

relations.label: Thoughts on the Present State of American Affairs

memes.label: Authority
memes.description: 'The rightful power to govern, which Paine argues derives solely from the consent of the governed rather than from heredity, scripture, or conquest'

memes.label: Battle of Bunker Hill
memes.description: 'The June 1775 battle near Boston; Paine argues its cost makes reconciliation impossible'

memes.label: Boston
memes.description: The Massachusetts port city at the center of colonial resistance; Paine

memes.label: Siege of Boston
memes.description: 'The 1775–1776 siege of British-occupied Boston; referenced in Common Sense as a symbol of British oppression'

memes.label: Boston Tea Party
memes.description: 'The 1773 protest against British tea taxes that triggered the Coercive Acts; the escalation point after which reconciliation became structurally impossible'

memes.label: British Admiralty
memes.description: 'The administrative body commanding the Royal Navy; referenced in Common Sense in the context of the naval blockade of American ports'

memes.label: British-Colonial Constitutional Conflict
memes.description: 'The broader constitutional crisis between Britain and its American colonies over the nature of imperial governance'

memes.label: British Constitution
memes.description: 'The system of government Paine critiques as a facade for monarchical tyranny, arguing its vaunted balance of King, Lords, and Commons is a disguise for two ancient tyrannies'

memes.label: British Crown
memes.description: 'The institution of the British monarchy; the primary target of Paine''s critique of hereditary rule'

memes.label: British East India Company
memes.description: 'The trading corporation whose tea was destroyed in the Boston Tea Party; referenced in Common Sense as an instrument of British commercial imperialism'

memes.label: House of Commons
memes.description: 'The lower house of the British Parliament; Paine acknowledges it as the sole republican element of the English constitution'

memes.label: House of Lords
memes.description: 'The upper house of the British Parliament, composed of hereditary peers; Paine condemns it as an aristocratic remnant'

memes.label: British Ministry
memes.description: 'The executive government of Great Britain, led by the Prime Minister; Paine argues its policies toward America are structurally oppressive'

memes.label: British Parliament
memes.description: 'The supreme legislative body of Great Britain; the target of Paine''s critique of virtual representation and colonial governance'

memes.label: Charles I
memes.description: 'King of England executed in 1649; Paine cites his fate as evidence that monarchy does not ensure stability'

memes.label: Charles II
memes.description: 'King of England, Scotland, and Ireland (1660–1685), addressed by the Quaker Robert Barclay and referenced in the Appendix'

memes.label: Charter
memes.description: 'The foundational document that defines and limits the powers of government, which Paine argues should be established by the people through a Continental Conference before any new government takes form'

memes.label: Church of England
memes.description: 'The established church of England; Paine critiques its entanglement with monarchical power'

memes.label: Civil Liberty
memes.description: 'The freedom of individuals from governmental oppression in their civic and political lives, which Paine treats as inseparable from religious liberty'

memes.label: Colonial Assemblies
memes.description: 'The elected legislative bodies of the American colonies; Paine saw them as the legitimate foundation for American self-government'

memes.label: Commerce
memes.description: 'Paine''s analysis of trade as both a practical argument for American independence and a moral concern — commerce with Britain enriches the empire at America''s expense while diminishing the spirit of the people'

memes.label: Consent of the Governed
memes.description: 'The Lockean principle that legitimate government derives its authority from the agreement of those it governs, which Paine deploys to delegitimize British rule over the American colonies'

memes.label: Constitution
memes.description: 'Paine''s argument for a written constitution as the foundation of legitimate government, established by the people through deliberate choice'

memes.label: Continental Congress
memes.description: 'The convention of delegates from the thirteen American colonies that served as the de facto national government during the Revolution; Paine''s primary audience and the vehicle for his constitutional vision'

memes.label: Custom
memes.description: 'The force of habitual thinking that Paine identifies as a barrier to reason and political progress'

memes.label: David
memes.description: 'Biblical king of Israel; Paine notes that scripture''s praise of David ignores his kingly office, focusing on his personal character instead'

memes.label: Divine Providence Debate
memes.description: 'The theological debate over whether American independence was consistent with divine providence; Paine''s Appendix refutes the providentialist case for submission to Britain'

memes.label: Dragonetti
memes.description: 'Italian political writer on virtue and rewards, cited by Paine as an authority on government'

memes.label: Duty
memes.description: 'The obligation that binds citizens to one another and to just government, which Paine distinguishes from the false duty of submission to hereditary authority'

memes.label: Edward IV
memes.description: 'Yorkist king during the Wars of the Roses; referenced in Paine''s catalog of hereditary succession''s bloody consequences'

memes.label: England
memes.description: The imperial power against which the American colonies revolted; Paine's

memes.label: English Civil War
memes.description: 'The 1642–1651 conflict between Parliament and the Crown; Paine cites it as evidence that monarchy breeds civil war'

memes.label: Equality
memes.description: 'Paine''s argument that all humans are naturally equal, making hereditary distinctions of rank illegitimate'

memes.label: Europe
memes.description: The continental frame of reference for Paine's political analysis of

memes.label: France
memes.description: 'The major European power frequently invoked by Paine as a counterweight to Britain'

memes.label: Freedom
memes.description: 'Paine''s pairing of freedom with security as the twin ends of legitimate government'

memes.label: George III
memes.description: 'King of Great Britain and Ireland (1760–1820), the unnamed central antagonist of *Common Sense*'

memes.label: Gideon
memes.description: 'Biblical judge of Israel; Paine cites Gideon as scriptural evidence that monarchy is contrary to divine will'

memes.label: Government
memes.description: 'Paine''s conception of government as a necessary evil, distinct from society, instituted to restrain vice and protect freedom'

memes.label: Happiness
memes.description: 'The positive human flourishing that Paine identifies as the proper end of society and the measure by which governments must be judged'

memes.label: Henry VI
memes.description: 'English king during the Wars of the Roses; Paine cites him as evidence of the bloodshed caused by disputed succession'

memes.label: Hereditary Succession
memes.description: 'Paine''s critique of hereditary succession as an irrational and unjust mechanism for transferring political power'

memes.label: Holland
memes.description: The Dutch Republic, cited by Paine as proof that a nation can prosper

memes.label: Honor
memes.description: 'A political and moral value that Paine redefines in republican terms, contrasting the empty honors of monarchy with the substantive honor of defending liberty and self-government'

memes.label: Parliamentary Representation Crisis
memes.description: 'The constitutional crisis over American representation in the British Parliament'

memes.label: House of Lancaster
memes.description: 'The rival house to York in the Wars of the Roses; both serve Paine''s argument against hereditary succession'

memes.label: House of York
memes.description: 'One of the rival dynastic houses in the Wars of the Roses; invoked by Paine as evidence of hereditary succession''s destructiveness'

memes.label: Independence
memes.description: 'Paine''s argument for American independence as the necessary and inevitable conclusion of the conflict with Britain'

memes.label: "Introduction"
memes.description: "Introduction to Common Sense by Thomas Paine"

memes.label: Justice
memes.description: 'The moral standard by which Paine judges political institutions, contrasted with the arbitrary will of monarchy and identified as the proper foundation of civil order'

memes.label: Law
memes.description: 'The system of rules that governs civil society, which Paine distinguishes from both arbitrary monarchical will and the higher standard of natural justice'

memes.label: Battles of Lexington and Concord
memes.description: 'The April 19, 1775 battles that began the American Revolution'

memes.label: Liberty
memes.description: 'Paine''s conception of liberty as the natural condition of mankind and the proper end of government'

memes.label: Lord North
memes.description: 'British Prime Minister (1770–1782) during the American Revolution, denounced in Paine''s "Common Sense" as part of "the whole detestable junto"'

memes.label: Marquess of Rockingham
memes.description: 'British statesman whose administration repealed the Stamp Act, cited in Paine''s "Common Sense" Appendix as an example of royal prerogative over Parliament'

memes.label: Massanello
memes.description: 'Neapolitan fisherman who led a 1647 revolt; Paine warns that failure to establish legitimate government invites such uncontrolled uprisings'

memes.label: Monarchy
memes.description: 'Paine''s critique of monarchy as an illegitimate form of government, originating in conquest and perpetuated by hereditary succession'

memes.label: Mr. Pelham
memes.description: 'British Prime Minister referenced by Paine as an able minister who nonetheless could not remedy the structural defects of monarchy'

memes.label: Natural Liberty
memes.description: 'The pre-political condition of humanity in which individuals are free but vulnerable; Paine uses it to explain why society and government arise and to define their legitimate limits'

memes.label: Natural Rights
memes.description: 'The rights inherent in all people by virtue of their existence, which Paine argues government must protect rather than grant'

memes.label: Nature
memes.description: 'The natural order of creation which Paine invokes as the normative standard for evaluating political institutions, in contrast to artificial arrangements like monarchy and hereditary succession'

memes.label: Necessity
memes.description: 'The principle that unavoidable circumstances compel political action, which Paine invokes to justify American independence as a matter of survival rather than choice'

memes.label: Norman Conquest
memes.description: 'The 1066 Norman invasion of England; the founding event of the English monarchy in Paine''s critique'

memes.label: Norman Dynasty
memes.description: 'The dynasty founded by William the Conqueror; for Paine, the original usurpation from which all English monarchy descends'

memes.label: Obedience
memes.description: 'The duty of citizens to obey government, which Paine argues is conditional upon the government serving the public good rather than upon tradition or fear'

memes.label: Oliver Cromwell
memes.description: 'Leader of the Parliamentary forces in the English Civil War and Lord Protector; referenced by Paine as an example of usurpation and the danger of conflating divine providence with political power'

memes.label: Oppression
memes.description: 'The unjust exercise of power by which a government or ruler crushes the liberty of the people, which Paine identifies as both a consequence of illegitimate government and a justification for resistance'

memes.label: Order
memes.description: 'The principle of structured arrangement that Paine invokes in multiple senses — natural order of equality, political order of government, and the civic order of obedience — to argue against hereditary rule'

memes.label: "Part 1: Of the Origin and Design of Government in General"
memes.description: "On the origin and design of government in general, with concise remarks on the English constitution"

memes.label: "Part 2: Of Monarchy and Hereditary Succession"
memes.description: "On monarchy and hereditary succession"

memes.label: "Part 3: Thoughts on the Present State of American Affairs"
memes.description: "Thoughts on the present state of American affairs"
aliases: Thoughts on the Present State of American Affairs

memes.label: "Part 4: Of the Present Ability of America"
memes.description: "Of the present ability of America, with some miscellaneous reflections"

memes.label: Peace
memes.description: 'The condition of non-conflict that Paine examines as both a desirable social state and a political outcome that monarchy fails to deliver, arguing that true peace requires independence'

memes.label: Pennsylvania
memes.description: 'One of the thirteen American colonies, referenced in Common Sense as an example of colonial self-governance and economic capacity.'

memes.label: Philadelphia
memes.description: The largest city in colonial America and the site where Common Sense

memes.label: Posterity
memes.description: 'Future generations whose rights and welfare Paine argues must guide present political decisions'

memes.label: Power
memes.description: 'The capacity to govern or control, which Paine argues must derive from the consent of the governed rather than hereditary claim'

memes.label: Prejudice
memes.description: 'The cognitive bias that Paine identifies as a primary obstacle to clear political reasoning, which he urges readers to set aside in favor of reason and common sense'

memes.label: 'Prerogative'
memes.description: 'Paine''s theological argument that the authority to establish and depose governments belongs exclusively to God, not to any earthly king or institution'

memes.label: Property
memes.description: 'Paine''s treatment of property as a natural right that government exists to protect, and the material stake that gives citizens an interest in political order'

memes.label: Quaker Meeting
memes.description: 'The local congregational body of Quakers; addressed by Paine in the Appendix of Common Sense'

memes.label: Quakers
memes.description: 'The Religious Society of Friends; Paine addresses them directly in the Appendix, urging them to support the revolutionary cause by appealing to their own principles (P01-001)'

memes.label: Reason
memes.description: 'The faculty of rational judgment that Paine elevates as the proper foundation for political argument, contrasting it with custom, tradition, and prejudice'

memes.label: Reconciliation
memes.description: 'Paine''s argument against reconciliation with Britain, framed as an impossible and self-defeating political strategy'

memes.label: Reconciliation Debate of 1775
memes.description: 'The political debate over whether the colonies should seek reconciliation with Britain or pursue independence'

memes.label: Religious Liberty
memes.description: 'The principle that individuals have a natural right to worship according to their own conscience, which Paine invokes to argue against religious tests and established churches'

memes.label: Representation
memes.description: 'Paine''s argument for equal representation as the mechanism through which the governed participate in their own governance'

memes.label: Republic
memes.description: 'Paine''s conception of a republic as government by elected representatives, the legitimate alternative to monarchy and hereditary rule'

memes.label: Resistance
memes.description: 'The justified opposition to tyrannical government, which Paine presents as both a natural right and a prudential necessity when government ceases to serve the public good'

memes.label: Revolution of 1688
memes.description: 'The 1688 overthrow of James II; implicitly referenced in Paine''s constitutional critique'

memes.label: Robert Barclay
memes.description: 'Scottish Quaker apologist whose address to Charles II is cited in the Appendix of Common Sense'

memes.label: Samuel Adams
memes.description: 'American revolutionary and Founding Father, organizer of resistance to British policy in Massachusetts'

memes.label: Samuel
memes.description: 'Biblical prophet who warned Israel against monarchy; Paine cites him alongside Gideon as divine witness against kingship'

memes.label: Saul
memes.description: 'First king of Israel; Paine notes Saul was chosen by lot, undermining the principle of hereditary succession'

memes.label: Security
memes.description: 'The protection of life, liberty, and property that Paine identifies as the true end and design of government'

memes.label: Seven Years' War
memes.description: 'The 1756–1763 global conflict that left Britain financially strained and led to colonial taxation'

memes.label: Sir John Dalrymple
memes.description: 'Scottish historian and political writer referenced in the Appendix of Common Sense'

memes.label: Sir William Meredith
memes.description: 'British MP who described English government as a republic; cited by Paine to expose contradictions in British constitutional theory'

memes.label: 'Slavery'
memes.description: 'Paine''s concept of political servitude — the loss of freedom through failed republican virtue, absence of law, or deceptive reconciliation'

memes.label: Social Contract
memes.description: 'The philosophical theory that government derives its legitimacy from the consent of the governed'

memes.label: Society
memes.description: 'Paine''s conception of society as the natural, positive product of human wants — distinct from and prior to government'

memes.label: Spain
memes.description: 'The European monarchy consistently paired with France in Paine''s strategic calculus against Britain'

memes.label: Stamp Act Repeal
memes.description: 'The 1766 repeal of the Stamp Act; referenced in the Appendix as an example of British inconsistency'

memes.label: "Subjects"
memes.description: "Table of contents from the original 1776 edition of Common Sense"

memes.label: Thomas Paine
memes.description: 'Author of *Common Sense* (1776), the pamphlet that galvanized American public opinion toward independence'

memes.label: Trust
memes.description: 'Paine''s concept of political trust as the conditional and revocable delegation of authority from the people to their governors, central to his argument against hereditary monarchy'

memes.label: Turkey
memes.description: 'The Ottoman Empire, invoked by Paine as an example of despotic government comparable to monarchy'

memes.label: Tyranny
memes.description: 'Paine''s analysis of tyranny as the corruption of government into arbitrary power, and the moral imperative to resist it'

memes.label: Usurpation
memes.description: 'The illegitimate seizure of political power, which Paine identifies as the origin of monarchy and the defining character of British rule over America'

memes.label: Virtue
memes.description: 'Paine''s use of virtue as a political concept — the moral quality that sustains republican government'

memes.label: War
memes.description: 'Paine''s analysis of war as a consequence of monarchy and the uncertain instrument of political contention, contrasted with the peace achievable through republican government'

memes.label: Wars of the Roses
memes.description: 'The 15th-century dynastic conflict between Lancaster and York; Paine''s prime example of hereditary succession''s bloody consequences'

memes.label: William Penn
memes.description: 'English Quaker and founder of Pennsylvania, referenced through the colony that bears his name'

memes.label: William the Conqueror
memes.description: 'The Norman duke who conquered England in 1066; Paine uses him as the archetypal example of monarchy''s illegitimate origin in conquest'
